title: With Narendra Modi taking lead, how BJP is appropriating Congress's issues, leaders
text: Nationalism, protection of cows from slaughter, eradicating poverty, economic reforms, digital technology are today political issues that most identify with the BJP, with Prime Minister Narendra Modi as champion. But none of these issues originally belonged to the BJP. They were all Congress's issues. When India got independence, the Congress was symbol of nationalism and talked about bringing development to the country. Read Full Story The Congress had been the ruling party at the Centre for 55 of nearly 73 years post-independence period. It won elections between 1951-52 and 1967 primarily on its nationalistic appeal. In 1971 Lok Sabha election, garibi hatao (eradicate poverty) was the poll slogan of Indira Gandhi's Congress. She got a thumping majority in the election. During the campaign, her common refrain was - "they say Indira hatao, I say garibi hatao". Incidentally, in 2019 Lok Sabha election, PM Modi told his audiences the same - "The Opposition is working on Modi hatao campaign while I am on garibi hatao mission". Like Indira Gandhi, PM Modi won a bigger majority in the polls. It was the Indira Gandhi government that first began economic reforms with an intent to integrate India's market with the world's. The economy

title: Why DK Shivakumar is the Congress's last resort
text: It’s a pun that’s been doing the political circles for some time now. DK Shivakumar is Congress’s last resort’. In the cess pit that is Indian politics the term horse trading’ is frequently used. And in this world where MLAs are frequently herded into resorts to shield them from poaching, Shivakumar is looked up in awe, a regent of resort politics. Read Full Story Who is Doddalahalli Kempegowda Shivakumar, 57, who wanted to have coffee with colleagues but was stopped by the Mumbai Police from doing so? For good reasons he is known as the Congress’s troubleshooter, even a kotwal (sentinel). He has taken on Bharatiya Janata Party’s master strategist Amit Shah twice in the recent past and come out trumps. Rewind to May 2018 after the Karnataka assembly results were declared. title: Why is Karnataka government always in jeopardy?
text: Nataka in Karnataka has become a norm rather than exception. Ever since the Karnataka election results threw a hung assembly, running a government in the state has been a challenging affair. Congress leader Siddaramaiah, who blames Prime Minister Narendra Modi and BJP chief Amit Shah for the current upheaval, on Tuesday told media that it was the BJP's sixth attempt to destablise Congress-Janata Dal (Secular) government in last 13 months. Read Full Story With unhappy MLAs consistently threatening to quit, the coalition government has been on edge ever since the formation of the government in May, 2018. But the drama really picked from December onwards when two independents MLAs withdrew support. They flip-flopped a lot but eventually came back into the fold. However, in February the Operations Kamal 3.0, as the Congress claims, was out in the open. Seven Congress MLAs and one JD (S) MLA skipped assembly sessions. Allegation flew as Congress accused BJP of kidnapping them. In another allegation the opposition party was accused of offering bribe to the tune of 30-40 crore to lure MLAs. Days later, the MLAs returned. They had been allegedly camping in a Mumbai hotel. Next month, Congress MLA Umesh Jadhav resigned to

title: BL Santhosh replaces Ram Lal: Why does RSS want greater control over BJP?
text: Ram Lal, the longest-serving Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP) general secretary (organisation) is back with the Rashtriya Swayamsevak Sangh (RSS). He was repatriated over the weekend after serving as bridge between the BJP and the RSS for 12 years. Ram Lal has been replaced by BL Santhosh, who is considered a more purist Sangh ideologue than his predecessor. Unlike Ram Lal, Santhosh is not known to hold back his views if a BJP leader or the party leadership deviates from the core RSS ideology of Hindutva. Read Full Story The development comes at a crucial time when there has been unease in the RSS over the organisation being "ignored" by the BJP leadership in giving credit for the Lok Sabha election result. It follows a national conclave of RSS prant pracharaks at Vijayawada in Andhra Pradesh ending on Saturday. The RSS conclave decided to increase "our effortsfor the purpose of social transformation." At the three-day conclave, the RSS leaders sounded uncomfortable with the BJP leaders giving total credit for the party's win to Prime Minister Narendra Modi's popularity and Union Home Minister Amit Shah's organisational skills. title: Karnataka: Yediyurappa gains from disqualification but there is a catch
text: Karnataka Assembly Speaker KR Ramesh Kumar on Sunday relieved Chief Minister BS Yediyurappa of a major headache by disqualifying remaining 14 rebel Congress-Janata Dal (Secular) MLAs. This was evident in Karnataka Assembly today as Yediyurappa government won confidence motion by voice vote. Karnataka Assembly is a 225-member house including one nominated anglo-Indian MLA. Speaker Ramesh Kumar has disqualified 17 of them. The total strength of Karnataka Assembly came down to 207 (Speaker excluded). Speaker can vote only in the case of a tie. Read Full Story This brought down the effective majority mark in Karnataka Assembly to 104. The BJP has 105 MLAs of its own and declared support of one Independent MLA, who was till recently a minister in HD Kumaraswamy government. Rebellion of the 18 coalition MLAs -- including the Independent -- brought down the Kumaraswamy government bringing Yediyurappa as the chief minister for the fourth time at 76. At the same time, the rebel MLAs were a big headache for him as he was, reportedly, under pressure to accommodate most of them in his council of ministers. This challenge is said to have forced him to take oath alone on Friday. Yediyurappa, BJP only beneficiaries The rebel

text: Politics is an art of constant realignment and building fresh narratives for public consumption. Prime Minister Narendra Modi and NCP chief Sharad Pawar are holding opposite fronts in politics today. At such a time came the surprise development of the Enforcement Directorate (ED) booking Sharad Pawar in a money laundering case. Sharad Pawar's nephew Ajit Pawar has also been named in connection with a bank scam and the Narendra Modi government stands accused of political vendetta. The case is based on an FIR filed by the Mumbai Police naming Ajit Pawar and 70 others in a case of malfeasance in the Maharashtra State Cooperative Bank. title: Onion bomb and Modi government's dilemma
text: The smell of Onion is in the air. The Onion bomb is ticking again. It has put the Narendra Modi government on the defensive as it tries to defuse the bomb before it explodes. Two back-to-back desperate attempts to tackle the onion crisis by the Modi government show how much importance the bulb holds in the country. The Modi government has imposed a complete ban on the export of onions. The decision was taken on Sunday apparently on a report submitted by a team sent to the Lasangaon mandi in Maharashtra to study the onion chain. Lasalgaon mandi is the biggest not only in terms of the volume of onions it controls but also as an influencer of prices. Read Full Story Before imposing a blanket ban on onion exports, the government had raised the minimum export price (MEP) for onions to $850 a tonne. It roughly translates into Rs 60 a kilo. It was as good as imposing a ban on onion exports as the competition offers better price. Going by the existing mechanism of onion trade, onion growers get a better price if they manage to export their produce in comparison to taking the vegetable to a mandi

title: Is Haryana Congress ensuring a BJP victory and return of Khattar?
text: Going against the conventional wisdom that dominated and sustained the Congress for long in politics, the party has put all its eggs in one basket in Haryana. The faction led by former Haryana Congress president Ashok Tanwar has been completely sidelined not only in deciding affairs related to the upcoming assembly election but also in ticket distribution. With this allegation, Ashok Tanwar led his supporters to stage a protest outside Congress president Sonia Gandhi's home in New Delhi. But taking Haryana Congress's internal political battle to New Delhi did not help his cause. The two lists -- of 84 and six candidates -- apparently do not feature Congress leaders considered close to Ashok Tanwar, who was the Haryana Congress president till a month ago when Kumari Selja replaced him. title: What will decide Maharashtra and Haryana assembly elections: Issues or narrative?
text: Maharashtra and Haryana assembly elections have entered their last lap. Campaign for assembly elections in both Maharashtra and Haryana has intensified. Top leaders of the ruling BJP, the Congress and other Opposition parties are holding rallies after rallies raising issues and trying to build favourable narrative. Issues of public importance dominate politics but elections are won on narrative. Issues in India - unemployment, unprofitable agriculture and access to quality education, robust healthcare system, drinking water etc -- have remained almost the same since the first election in 1951-52. Still governments have changed or returned to power in states and at the Centre despite their performance. Read Full Story A similar build up of 'issues versus narrative' is being seen in Maharashtra and Haryana, two states that will vote on October 21 to elect their next governments. The two states have much in common. Both are the BJP-ruled and in both the states the BJP came to power after a long gap. Maharashtra and Haryana are among the more developed states with per capita income better than most. title: Is two-child policy a realistic goal in India?
text: Debate on two-child policy is back in India courtesy the Bharatiya Janata Party government of Assam which has decided to implement a law passed two years ago. Under the two-child policy, no person having more than two children would be eligible for government jobs in Assam from January 2021. The law, Population and Women Empowerment Policy of Assam, was passed by the Assam Assembly in September 2017. Besides putting a two-child norm for government employment on new job seekers, the policy also calls for strict adherence by the existing government staff. Read Full Story The story of population control through family planning is a curious case in India, which holds the distinction of being the first among the developing countries to officially launch a family planning programme as early as 1951. The first Lok Sabha had not yet been constituted. India elected its 17th Lok Sabha earlier this year. Family planning centre to family expansion centre The policy to control expansion of population in 1951 was revolutionary in idea particularly when seen in the background of Mahatma Gandhi's disapproval of such a government-backed campaign. Gandhi opposed the idea of contraception to control family size. He supported the idea of abstinence

title: Were Dalit votes key to Congress's comeback in Haryana assembly elections?
text: Five years ago when the Congress contested Haryana Assembly election, it lost badly. Haryana was among the first Congress citadels to fall after the Modi wave ended 10-year-rule of the alliance led by the grand-old-party. Soon, other Congress bastions fell one-by-one. In 2014, Congress lost Haryana after two consecutive terms in government. In the 90-member assembly, it won just 15 seats and had a vote share of 20.58 per cent. Read Full Story Five years on, the Congress has won 31 seats and secured a vote share of 28.1 per cent. Though this time the Congress has managed to double its seat tally from what it won five years ago, the party is far from its halcyon days in Haryana. But the story of Congress's comeback in Haryana appears to have been factored on how Dalits voted in this election. An indication of this comes from the voting patterns in 17 seats reserved for the Scheduled Castes in Haryana. Of these 17 seats, the BJP has won 5, the Congress 7 and Others 5. Among Others, JJP has won 4 seats. Five years ago, the BJP had won 9 of the 17 reserved seats in Haryana, while Congress and Others

title: BJP-Shiv Sena tussle in Maharashtra: Until election did oldest allies part
text: What has happened in Maharashtra post-election is a rarity in India. Usually, parties falling short of majority have tended to cobble up alliances, at times, with ideologically or functionally opposite parties. The BJP allied with the PDP in Jammu and Kashmir, Nitish Kumar's JDU joined hands with Lalu Prasad's RJD in Bihar, the Aam Aadmi Party (AAP) formed its first government with support from the Congress, and even the Congress stitched up alliance with the JDS in Karnataka. Read Full Story In Maharashtra, BJP and Shiv Sena contested the recently-held election jointly. They sought vote for the ruling alliance saying theirs was oldest and natural alliance. When they were voted to power, the Shiv Sena and the BJP diverged on opposite routes over some "50-50" formula of power-sharing that was never put before the voters. It lies in the domain of ifs and buts what would have been the election result had the voters known the secret "50-50" understanding between the BJP and Shiv Sena, as the latter has claimed. The voters assumed they were in agreement about forming government together and they voted for continuation of the government giving a clear majority unlike 2014 when they had contested separately.

title: Jharkhand election: Women, tribal voters to decide who will be chief minister
text: When the BJP won 2014 Jharkhand Assembly election, not too many people expected Raghubar Das to be the Jharkhand chief minister. Reason: He was an outsider, originally belonging to Chhattisgarh, and he was an OBC leader. Jharkhand had always had a tribal leader as the chief minister till that point. But Raghubar Das got the nomination from the BJP for being a founder member of the party and having a good "equation" with Prime Minister Narendra Modi and BJP president Amit Shah, now also the Union home minister. Read Full Story Will Raghubar Das return as the chief minister of Jharkhand after the five-phase assembly election between November 30 and December 20? The answer largely lies in the manner in which women and tribal voters of Jharkhand exercise their franchise. That women voters could be a deciding factor in an election was not very well understood by the political parties till JDU leader Nitish Kumar showed it in neighbouring Bihar in 2015 assembly election. title: Why BJP lost Jharkhand Assembly election
text: Jharkhand Assembly election fits a pattern witnessed in past one year or so. The BJP government in a state has to perform well to return despite a favourable consideration for Prime Minister Narendra Modi. We have seen that if Narendra Modi is not a direct factor in an election, the BJP has struggled. It was evident in Punjab in 2017, in three Hindi-heartland states of Rajasthan, Madhya Pradesh and Chhattisgarh, and also in Haryana and Maharashtra recently. In Uttar Pradesh election in 2017, Narendra Modi was a factor. He is an MP from Varanasi in Uttar Pradesh and the BJP was not seeking a return to power in the state. It was seeking fresh mandate for itself. Read Full Story In Jharkhand, like in Haryana, Maharashtra, Rajasthan, Madhya Pradesh and Chhattisgarh, the BJP was seeking a returning mandate. Narendra Modi was not a direct factor. Only six months ago, Jharkhand had given a favourable mandate to Narendra Modi in the Lok Sabha election. text: In a move that has stunned the Opposition and political pundits, the Bharatiya Janata Party-led Centre announced it will conduct a caste census. The Congress party, of course, is taking credit for the move, saying that the saffron party conceded to relentless pressure from Rahul Gandhi. BJP supporters, on the other hand, are calling this move a masterstroke, claiming it has taken the sting out of the Opposition's main poll plank ahead of the Bihar Assembly polls. Read Full Story Regardless, the move came as a surprise as the BJP has long been non-committal about it. It has consolidated a Hindu-majority vote bank on the lines of Hindutva and nationalism, and exhorted voters to rise above caste. text: A fresh Mood of the Nation survey conducted by C-Voter for India Today suggests that dismissing Prime Minister Narendra Modi as a waning force was premature. Despite setbacks in the June 2024 Lok Sabha elections, PM Modi remains the most popular leader, with the NDA holding a major lead over the I.N.D.I. Alliance in projected seat share. In contemporary times, public discourse and narratives may not change overnight, but the pace remains relentless. Back in June 2024, nearly every political observer grappled with one question: Is this the beginning of the end of Brand Modi? The results of the Lok Sabha elections - where the BJP's tally dropped from 303 to 240 seats - delivered a severe blow to a party that had confidently championed the slogan "Abki Baar, 400 Paar." text: In February 2015, a friend of mine voted for the Aam Aadmi Party (AAP) in the Delhi assembly elections. All her life, she had been a steadfast Congress supporter: the first time she switched was in the 2014 general elections when she plumped for the BJP because she was angry at the ‘scam’ culture in the Manmohan Singh government. Now, months later, she had chosen to vote for AAP. “I like both Modi and Kejriwal,” she told me, adding: “They are self-made politicians who are promising change.” Read Full Story Fast-forward to February 2025. This time, my friend decided to vote for the BJP. For ten years, she stuck to the ‘Delhi formula’: Modi for PM, Kejriwal for CM. The Congress was not on her radar because she felt it was a ‘wasted vote’ since they had no chance of winning. So, why had she chosen not to vote for AAP this time? “Because I felt let down. title: Opinion: What voter turnout in 2025 Delhi polls could mean
text: The numbers aren't final. Yet. Early estimates shared by the Election Commission of India indicate that the final voter turnout in the 2025 Delhi Assembly elections will not be lower than the 62.6 per cent registered in 2020. In these polarised times, the data will be interpreted in a partisan manner till February 8 when the actual results will stop all speculation and lead to a postmortem. Till then, people will argue over whether a good voter turnout is advantage Aam Aadmi Party (AAP) or advantage Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP). Read Full Story The first lesson from recent history is that lower or higher voter turnouts are no sure indicators of pro- or anti-incumbency. Since December 2023, Assembly elections have been organised in six states: Chhattisgarh, Madhya Pradesh, Rajasthan, Jharkhand, Haryana, and Maharashtra. Of the six, electors delivered anti-incumbency verdicts in just two states, Rajasthan and Chhattisgarh. The other four delivered decisive pro-incumbency verdicts. Nothing conclusive can be drawn by examining voter turnouts compared to the previous election. title: Opinion: Can BJP repeat its Haryana-Maharashtra performance in Delhi?
text: The Delhi Assembly election is not business as usual for the three main players. For the Aam Aadmi Party in power for 10 years, it's an existential battle - a loss this time will push back its national expansion plans. For the Bharatiya Janata Party, out of power for more than two decades, a loss would highlight its weakness in fighting regional satraps. For the Indian National Congress, a rout again would expose the party's need to first solve structural issues within. All three need this win. Read Full Story Repeat of Haryana and Maharashtra? The Congress, led by the Hoodas, was the favourite in Haryana after winning five of its 10 seats in the Lok Sabha election and the state epicentre of wrestlers, farmers, and Agniveer protests. In Maharashtra too, with the Maha Vikas Aghadi winning 30 of the 48 Lok Sabha seats, the BJP-led Mahayuti was on the back foot. However, the BJP won a record third term in Haryana. And in Maharashtra, the Mahayuti won a record three-fourths majority. This was possible through micromanagement, small nukkad sabhas, and seat-by-seat contests with the Rashtriya Swayamsevak Sangh seeking votes. title: Akhilesh-Jayant’s bonhomie campaign: Pull young crowd, take Yogi head-on
text: In the rural parts of Agra, at a cold storage site, it’s a jammed scenario. A large banner seeking support for the local Samajwadi Party-Rashtriya Lok Dal alliance candidate is hanging from the roof of the tin shade. A majority of those who had thronged to this site were youngsters. While hundreds were surrounding the red bus an election rath standing in the middle of the compound, many more hundreds were sitting on the rooftop and walls of the cold storage site. Each of them looked enthusiastically towards the two leaders standing atop the red bus. Akhilesh Yadav and Jayant Choudhary. Read Full Story Every time Yadav would look in a specific direction, the youngster would cheer in sheer enthusiasm. And respond to the former Uttar Pradesh’s Chief Minister’s electoral banters. When the duo Yadav and Choudhary waved towards the supporters standing in the compound, they would try to move closer to the bus like a storm in the sea. Making it even more difficult for the security personnel and the media personnel --waiting from the SP-RLD chief to come down—to maintain their positions. title: Increased participation to political exclusion: The women factor in UP polls
text: Women voters have emerged in recent state elections not only as a distinct segment of the electorate but also in some cases as makers of elections. In West Bengal, last year, the Trinamool Congress led by an impressive 13 percent over BJP among women, according to the Lokniti-CSDS survey. In the 2020 Delhi elections, women were more likely to have voted for AAP than BJP by 11% (60% to 49%). In both cases, women voters transformed victories into landslides. Read Full Story In Northern India, there are states like Bihar where the BJP-JD(U) attracted more women than its main opponent, and states like Uttar Pradesh where men and women tend to vote for the same parties in equal proportion (according to the Lokniti-CSDS 2019 National Election Survey). Beyond these gendered variations in electoral preferences, women are also able to impact electoral outcomes through their increased participation. In the last electoral cycle (since 2017), women have outvoted men in 24 states. title: The Owaisi factor in UP election
text: It is a well-argued case that competing communal forces feed on each other to spread their area of influence. The same is on display in the ongoing assembly election in Uttar Pradesh. The provocative speeches made by Hindu extremists at Haridwar and elsewhere, the attack by two Hindu zealots on firebrand Member of Parliament Asaduddin Owaisi’s vehicle, and the hijab (veil) controversy a couple of days before the first phase of polling in Uttar Pradesh all these were apparently aimed at inciting communal tension to benefit electorally. But because of a tepid response from the Muslim community, they have failed. But Azam Khan has been out of the scene for quite some time as he is incarcerated. In such a situation, Owaisi, who is the president of the All India Majlis-e-Ittehadul Muslimeen (AIMIM) and a radical advocate of Muslim cause, jumped into the fray in UP in a big way. title: Exit poll predicts BJP winning more than it expected
text: Predicting the minds of Uttar Pradesh voters comes with a statutory 'hazard' warning. They are wizards at practising the art of franchise and over the last few polls, they have mastered the art of disguising their final choice at the time of pressing the EVM button. After travelling in UP for almost three months, I returned with a feeling that the hunter Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP) has become the hunted. So large and diverse was the BJP's 2017 victory cake that it seemed the party was failing to stop opponents from nibbling away like rats the pieces that made the cake. There were too many negatives which voters from west to east and north to south were extremely vocal about. Read Full Story That’s why the Axis-My India exit poll handing over 288-326 win share to the BJP was a surprise. The exit poll showed the opposition parties like the Samajwadi Party (SP) trailing far behind with 71-101 seats and the Bahujan Samaj Party (BSP) with 3-9 seats. title: Why more women voted for the BJP in 2022 elections
text: The story of women voting in India has added one more puzzle for social scientists to decipher. In the past decade, the research largely focused on the increasing voter registration among women and their higher turnout rate at polling booths. The combined effect of these two developments has been that in many parts of the country, the electoral weight of women voters is higher than men. In addition to these, women voters have also been making distinct preferences in vote choice. In the past few years, we have seen the positive effect of this behind the re-election of many Chief Ministers Nitish Kumar, Mamata Banerjee, J Jayalalitha, Naveen Patnaik, among others. advertisement ❮❯ Read Full Story So what role did women voters play in BJP’s victory in four states? The data indicates that more women seem to have voted for the BJP than men in all four states the party won; both the Axis My-India and Lokniti-CSDS post-poll survey data confirm this pattern. While the margin of difference is in the range of 2-4 percentage points, the importance of this turnaround should not be discounted. The BJP used to have a gender disadvantage with women before 2014, but the party

The war cry charged up the BJP cadres to wrest power that had eluded them for 10 years. Since then, the BJP has expanded its base, dislodging the Congress in state after state. Read Full Story Today, about a decade later, the BJP has 12 chief ministers, and the party shares power in six other states. The number of Congress chief ministers has reduced to two, and there are only three states where it is part of the ruling coalition as a junior partner. text: Losing an election is no big deal, especially for veterans. It’s part of the game. They win, they lose, and they move on. Victories and defeats notwithstanding, their clout in the corridors of power remains. Generally. In Uttarakhand, Harish Rawat is one such politician—a veteran of several defeats and some victories. In a political career spanning over four decades, he has contested nine Lok Sabha elections and won four of them. At the state level, he entered the electoral fray on four occasions but won just once, that too in a by-election when the Congress was comfortably in power. Read Full Story Despite this not-so-glittering electoral performance, Harish Rawat remains a towering politician in Uttarakhand. title: SM Krishna: A lawyer, scholar, politician and CEO Chief Minister who built Brand Bangalore
text: Somanahalli Mallaiah Krishna (SM Krishna, for short), one of the rare politicians from Karnataka who seamlessly traversed between national and state politics for close to six decades, discharging multiple responsibilities, is perhaps best remembered for building ‘Brand Bengaluru’ during his tenure as the chief minister of Karnataka from 1999 to 2004. In the mid-1980s, the launching of Infosys and Wipro Technologies in Bangalore by two pioneers, Narayana Murthy and Azim Premji, heralded the beginning of an “Information Technology revolution” in the country. They began to take wings in the early 1990s, going in for large public funding and out-sourcing operations to multinational companies abroad. Bangalore was seen as a “natural choice” for the IT sector because of the number of engineers it churned out every year, its salubrious climate which attracted talented workers from across the country and the state’s own pro-industry policies. Read Full Story He ended policy paralysis But, the political instability that preceded Krishna taking charge as chief minister in October 1999, after leading the Congress party to a handsome majority, had nearly ruined Karnataka’s “dream projects.” The rag-tag Janata Dal government led by JH Patel which came to power after HD Deve Gowda moved to Delhi

title: Narendra Modi vs Opposition in states over CAA-NPR, NIA
text: In his first term as the prime minister, Narendra Modi had tough time in communicating with the Opposition. Trust deficit between the ruling and the Opposition parties became more evident than before. Except the Goods and Services Tax (GST) legislation, no major policy or executive decision of the Modi government has got any support from the Opposition camp. The Citizenship Amendment Act (CAA) has brought the Opposition and the Modi government on a collision path. Read Full Story States have been passing resolutions (Kerala, Punjab and Rajasthan, plus West Bengal) against the CAA. Kerala has moved the Supreme Court challenging the constitutional validity of the CAA. Besides CAA, another bone of contention between the Modi government and the Opposition-ruled states seems to be the National Investigation Agency (NIA). A petition by Congress-ruled Chhattisgarh is already pending in the Supreme Court against the NIA law. Maharashtra, too, is weighing its options against the Centre over NIA's jurisdiction. But before we have a look at what is happening in the case of CAA and NPR (National Population Register), let us see how Modi versus Opposition build up has taken place. ASSERTIVE MODI VS EDGY OPPOSITION Smarting under Prime Minister Narendra Modi's capacity

title: Is Shaheen Bagh a success? How Modi govt has climbed down on CAA-NRC
text: On December 30, Prime Minister Narendra Modi posted a video clip showing spiritual debater Jaggi Vasudev, popularly known as Sadhguru, to ramp up support for the Citizenship Amendment Act (CAA) that had sparked widespread protests across the country. Sadhguru defends the CAA in the video but also asserts that the Modi government was caught napping as protesters took to streets, most prominently at Shaheen Bagh in New Delhi. Read Full Story The anti-CAA protests continue unabated despite a definite climbdown by the government on CAA-NRC (National Register of Citizens) project, a favourite of the ruling BJP. The pressure on the government has been so immense that PM Modi chose to publicly overrule his Home Minister Amit Shah, arguably the second-most powerful person in the government and the BJP. The CAA-NRC project was part of the BJP manifesto for 2019 Lok Sabha election. title: Message from Delhi election results: Public hai sab jaanti hai
text: When asked to respond to Delhi Assembly election results, Bihar Chief Minister and Janata Dal United president Nitish Kumar said, "Janata maalik hai (public is the lord)." Nitish Kumar's JDU failed to win a single seat in Delhi election that it fought in alliance with its senior NDA partner, the BJP. The two parties will be facing public later this year when Nitish Kumar will seek yet another term as Bihar CM. Read Full Story The "janata maalik hai" remark appeared to be at variance with the narrative that the nation is in the grip of a Modi wave, as the BJP continues to assert in victory and in defeats. The "janata maalik hai" presents the voters and people of the country as wise women who make informed choices in elections. The wave theory on the other hand conveys that the janata is under the influence of some spell of magic. Delhi Assembly election fits in the assessment that gives the devil - the janata - its due. Appearance of Narendra Modi on the national scene and 2019 Lok Sabha election marked the beginning of a new phase of politics and electioneering in India. People voted a single-party majority government

The question is not only important for the Congress party but also for national politics as it is the largest Opposition party in Parliament and has an obligation to act as the conscience-keeper of the government. title: What Balakot air strike achieved in one year
text: To avenge the Pulwama terror attack in Jammu and Kashmir, India conducted an aerial strike at a terror training centre in Balakot of Pakistan's Khyber Pakhtunkhwa province on February 26 last year. Forty soldiers were killed in the terror attack claimed by Pakistan-based terror outfit Jaish-e-Mohammed on February 14. This was the second major cross-LoC/border response from India in the aftermath of a major terror attack in less than three years. The first such response was the surgical strike across the Line of Control in Pakistan-occupied Kashmir (PoK) in 2016 days after terror attack at Uri base camp of the Indian Army. Read Full Story Despite claims of killing hundreds of terror recruits, the scale of achievements in the Balakot air strike has remained in the realm of debate. Still, the action marked another reference point in India-Pakistan with the potential of influencing domestic politics in both the countries. TOUGH MESSAGING India's response to Pulwama terror attack was not anticipated by Pakistan. This became obvious with the relative ease the Indian Air Force carried out its mission. Pakistan Air Force was not ready to respond. In fact, it did not get any idea till the Indian team returned safely leaving

title: Rajya Sabha election: Where extra player changes rules of game
text: The election to the Rajya Sabha is indirect. The directly elected MLAs in the state assembly send their -- and thereby the people's -- representatives to the Rajya Sabha. Since the number of MLAs are fixed in the state assemblies and secured by the anti-defection law, the Rajya Sabha elections have rather been a boring exercise for political observers, except for the issue of who gets the party tickets. Read Full Story This is why most of the time, Rajya Sabha elections have seen candidates getting elected unopposed to the upper house of Parliament. The parties certainly winning the Rajya Sabha seat on account of number of MLAs would put up exactly the same number of candidates. In sum total, the final number of candidates of all parties equalled the total number of vacancies from the state. There was no race for snatching another’s seat in a Rajya Sabha election. title: Eye on 2023? How Gehlot outsmarted Pilot in Rajasthan Congress
text: It is highly uncommon in Indian politics that a chief minister and a deputy minister get notices from the police to record their statement, more so when the government is headed by a party enjoying majority. The trigger for Rajasthan political crisis was one such notice over an alleged attempt to topple the government. The charges mentioned in the FIR included those under IPC Sections 124-A and 120-B - sedition and conspiracy respectively. Now, it has emerged that the instruction for sending the notices, by the Special Operation Group (SOG) of the Rajasthan Police, came from Chief Minister Ashok Gehlot. An unsure SOG team had to re-confirm the same before actually pasting the notice at Sachin Pilot's residence. Read Full Story Gehlot holds the home portfolio. Days before the July 10 summons, Sachin Pilot had reached out to the party high command in New Delhi sensing that he could be "targeted" by Gehlot. But a "no response" from New Delhi forced Sachin Pilot to rebel. Some observers have claimed that that rebellion of Sachin Pilot is a calculated move by Gehlot. This move has a long back story. Like any other state, caste plays a key role in Maharashtra politics. title: Opinion: Ladki Bahin
text: In the next month, as the high-stakes Maharashtra elections takes centre stage, two words will dominate most conversations: ‘Ladki Bahin’. The flagship government scheme which provides Rs 1,500 per month to women in households whose annual income is less than Rs 2,50,000 has become a major talking point and raises the crucial political question: will the monies that have been put in the bank accounts of more than 2 crore women since August this year be enough to shake off any anti-incumbency against the ruling Mahayuti alliance in the state? Read Full Story This isn’t the first time governments at the state or centre across parties have offered pre-poll sops in the form of cash hand-outs to specific groups. But never before have the pay-out deliveries been so sudden, brazen and unmindful of financial consequences just before an important election. By the time voting is done next month, a majority of the beneficiary women will have received Rs 7,500 in their account, including a pre-Diwali ‘bonus’ announced just before the election code of conduct came into effect, with a monthly price tag of approximately Rs 3,700 crore or around Rs 46,000 crore annually. title: Election 2020: America’s race problem
text: It’s being called the summer of racial reckoning in America and started earlier this year with the death of an African-American man in the midwestern city of Minneapolis at the hands of a white police officer. George Floyd’s death propelled nationwide protests across the US and also around the world against racial injustice. The killing was captured on video by bystanders in which he is seen pinned to the ground by police officers with one of them pressing his knee on Mr. Floyd’s neck. He pleads that he can't breathe but the officer doesn’t let go. Soon after, Mr. Floyd becomes unresponsive and is declared dead at a hospital. Read Full Story The deaths of Black Americans at the hands of the police are not new but the almost eight-minute-long video of George Floyd’s life slowly drifting away from him as the kneeling police officer brazenly looks at the phone cameras pointing at him and with his hands in his pocket - is a difficult one to watch. There seems a casual cold-bloodedness to the act. It touched a chord and prompted people to stage the biggest protests against racial inequality in decades. The police officer in Mr. Floyd’s case

title: Bihar election: Can Owaisi's poll engineering with Kushwaha challenge Nitish Kumar?
text: Bihar Chief Minister Nitish Kumar is seeking his fourth straight electoral victory in the Bihar Assembly election 2020. Among other rivals, the Bihar Assembly election this time sees a new experiment -- by two community-based parties led by Hyderabad MP Asaduddin Owaisi and former Union minister Upendra Kushwaha -- challenging Nitish Kumar in his quest to win a fresh mandate. Asaduddin Owaisi and Upendra Kushwaha have partnered to form the the Grand Democratic Secular Front. The coalition has decided to contest all 243 seats. Read Full Story Owaisi's All India Majlis-e-Ittehad-ul-Muslimeen (AIMIM), Kushwaha's Rashtriya Lok Samta Party (RLSP), the Bahujan Samaj Party (BSP) of Mayawati, Samajwadi Janata Dal (SJD) of former Union minister Devendra Prasad, the Janwadi Party (Socialist) of Dr Sanjay Chauhan and the Suheldeo Bharatiya Samaj Party of Om Prakash Rajbhar are constituents of the coalition. The AIMIM banks on Muslim votes for its electoral success. The RLSP drives its political power from its reach in the Kushwaha-Koeri-Kurmi community. These three castes are often spoken of in the same social-electoral bandwidth in Bihar. The BSP calls itself the champion of the Dalit cause. Similarly, the other parties of the coalition have limited political appeal in Bihar. CASTE CALCULUS

text: First, it was Sunny Deol and now it is Tejashwi Yadav’s turn to create history. In the complex and paradoxical world of political dynasties and electoral success, Tejashwi, if crowned as the chief minister of Bihar on November 10, 2020, will hold a unique distinction of being the third head of the state from the same family. Lalu Yadav served as the chief minister twice from 1990 to 1997 while Tejashwi’s mother Rabri Devi occupied the hot seat thrice between 1997 to 2005. Read Full Story In the world of abhinetas turning netas, Deols have a record of sending three members to parliament, all directly elected to the Lok Sabha. Actor Dharmendra won from Bikaner in the 2004 Lok Sabha elections while his wife Hema Malini is currently MP from Mathura. Dharmendra’s son from his other wife Prakash, Sunny Deol is a BJP MP from Gurdaspur. Bachchans too have had three parliamentarians -- poet Harivanshrai, Amitabh and Jaya Bachchan but only one from the Lok Sabha. title: Trump versus Biden election: What America needs to learn from India
text: Make America Great Again. This has been incumbent American President Donald Trump’s election battle cry. The world’s oldest modern democracy holds elections in a manner that may not qualify as “free” and “fair” in many other democracies. Certainly not in India. Here, the Election Commission has set such a high standard that its observers are called to some fledgling democracies of Africa to give elections there credibility. Read Full Story Let’s see first how the US conducts its election to arguably the most powerful office in the world to understand why the American presidential election -- where Joe Biden of the Democratic Party has challenged Donald Trump of the Republican Party -- has become controversial. Even before the first vote was cast, there were talks about the election being contested in the courts. AN AMERICAN ELECTION The US has a Federal Election Commission but it does not conduct elections in America . Its job is to regulate financing in election campaigns. title: Not votes in election but top lawyers in courts, what Congress offers allies
text: That the Congress has come far away from its halcyon days, days when it was the 'mover and shaker' of Indian politics, is now well established. Year after year, election after election. This was reiterated once again in the just-concluded Bihar assembly election where the Congress emerged as the weakling in the Mahagathbandhan, winning only 19 of 70 seats it contested. Read Full Story Its inability to pull votes is well known; its leadership's reluctance to do serious soul-searching is evident; and its stiff opposition and clampdown on any criticism from within the party was demonstrated not far too long ago. If in doubt, ask the 23 signatories to the letter that sought a complete leadership overhaul in the party recently. Nationally, the Congress is still the principal opposition party, both numerically and perception wise. It has failed to win enough seats in two consecutive Lok Sabha elections to even have the status of Leader of Opposition, but since there isn't any other competitor for the second slot, it by default becomes the principal opposition. The problem is more entrenched in the states. Especially in those that have strong regional parties and where the contest is not a direct one

title: With Vetrivel Yatra and Alagiri alliance, BJP plans its future in Tamil Nadu
text: The good showing in the Bihar Assembly election has given a shot in the BJP's arm in Tamil Nadu. Like Bihar, Tamil Nadu's politics is also on the cusp of a generational shift. Next year's Tamil Nadu Assembly election will be the first electoral battle without the familiar old stalwarts -- M Karunanidhi and J Jayalalithaa, both actors-turned-politicians who passed away since the last state polls held in 2016. Read Full Story Tamil Nadu politics, to put in context, is in flux. The BJP is trying to brew a fresh political soup in Tamil Nadu by laying claim to the political legacy of both the mainstream parties -- the DMK and the AIADMK. The BJP recently showcased AIADMK founder MG Ramachandran, the former Tamil Nadu chief minister and Jayalalithaa's mentor, in saffron colours as part of its Vetrivel Yatra. And, now it is eyeing an alliance with Karunanidhi's elder son MK Alagiri. J Jayalalithaa and M Karunanidhi dominated Tamil Nadu politics for about three decades together. title: Importance of being Suvendu Adhikari in Bengal’s stormy politics
text: Why is the Trinamool Congress trying to pacify, and the BJP attempting to woo, both aggressively, a sulking Suvendu Adhikari? The answer probably lies in the 65 Assembly seats spread across six districts of West Bengal where the Adhikari family holds sway. This is more than a fifth of the total 294 seats in the state. With elections in Bengal barely months away, Mamata Banerjee can ill afford to lose her heavyweight minister of Nandigram fame. The BJP, on the other hand, has smelled blood. Read Full Story THE RISE OF SUVENDU Suvendu comes from the politically influential Adhikari family of East Midnapore district. His father Sisir Adhikari was a Congress MLA from Kanthi Dakshin in 1982, but later became one of the founding members of the Trinamool Congress. At present, he is a three-time MP from Kanthi since 2009. Suvendu won the Kanthi Dakshin Assembly seat for Trinamool in 2006. Three years later, he was elected from the Tamluk Parliamentary seat. But in between lies his claim to fame. In 2007, Suvendu organised an anti-land acquisition movement against an Indonesian chemical company in Nandigram in East Midnapore district. title: BJP goes for Muslim-Christian social engineering in Kerala local election
text: A few days back, senior BJP leader and Karnataka minister KS Easwarappa created a flutter with a stereotype “no ticket to Muslims” remark in the upcoming Lok Sabha bypoll in Belagavi constituency. However, the BJP has no qualms in fielding more than 100 Muslim candidates in the panchayat polls in neighbouring Kerala. The elections to local bodies -- panchayats, municipalities and corporations -- will be held in Kerala on December 8, 10 and 14. The BJP has fielded 500 Christian and 112 Muslim candidates for election to panchayats, block panchayats and district panchayats. And, among those seeking votes for these candidates would be Union Home Minister Amit Shah and Uttar Pradesh Chief Minister Yogi Adityanath. Read Full Story This comes as part of the BJP’s outreach to the minority communities in Kerala. Muslims and Christians roughly comprise 45 per cent of Kerala’s population. title: Congress pot simmers again over talk of 'G 23' meet with Sonia
text: Speculation is rife over 'G 23' Congress dissenters getting an audience with party's interim chief Sonia Gandhi. News of an Ashok Gehlot visit to the national Capital on Friday has deepened the swirl in the party that something related to the Congress organisation and the Rajasthan impasse is coming up. The meet, if and when it materialises, would have far-reaching consequences on the fate of the Grand Old Party. After two subsequent general election washouts and a pervading malaise, matters came to a head in August when 23 senior leaders of the Congress sent a letter to Sonia calling for sweeping changes in the party, sparking an existential crisis. text: The Election Commission of India has blown the poll bugle for Maharashtra and Jharkhand. With scores tied 1-1 for the National Democratic Alliance and the INDIA bloc in the semifinals — recently concluded Haryana and Jammu and Kashmir Assembly polls — the finals promise to be a cracker of a contest. title: Opinion: Decoding Haryana poll conundrum
text: The BJP has won Haryana for a record third time, shocking political pundits and pollsters. It was a close election with BJP marginally ahead of Congress by less than one per cent vote share. While Congress gained 11 per cent vote share compared to 2019, BJP gained around three per cent. However, the saffron party romped home comfortably defeating Congress by 11 seats. Read Full Story This has led to a lot of debate on traditional and social media. How come despite similar vote shares and gaining significantly compared to previous polls, the Congress ended up on the losing side, in an election it was touted as favourite to win? We have had instances in the past where parties securing more votes have ended up winning fewer seats and losing the elections. In 2018 in Madhya Pradesh, BJP bagged 41.6 per cent vote share and won 109 seats while INC bagged 41.5 per cent vote share but won 114 seats. In Odisha earlier this year, BJD bagged 40.22 per cent vote share and won 51 seats while BJP bagged lesser vote share of 40.07 per cent but won the state with 78 seats. India operates on first past the post

title: Haryana Shocker: A BJP win, a Congress self-goal
text: If the 2024 Lok Sabha election surprised India, the Haryana 2024 verdict has shocked many. Rarely has there been an election where every pollster, every pundit, every reporter has failed to predict the outcome so clearly. So how did the BJP, written off by everyone, including privately by some of their own leaders, pull off a stunning upset against the odds and, in the face of a 10-year anti-incumbency score, a remarkable hat trick? Read Full Story Here are 10 key factors. 1) Indian politics is a first past the post system in which wafer-thin margins in vote share can have a disproportionate impact on seats. Haryana, a small state, is a classic example. Consider this: the Congress vote share actually increased by 11 per cent compared to 2019 from 28 per cent to 39 per cent. The BJP vote share increased by little over three per cent from 36.4 to 39.9 per cent. Both parties had roughly an identical vote share, but the BJP won 48 seats and the Congress 37. text: Two politically uncomfortable facts stand out in the aftermath of the alleged scandal over laddus in the famous Tirupati temple in Andhra Pradesh. Both could outwardly appear paradoxical. Both facts or sentiments were visible in a nationwide survey conducted by CVoter after the revelation that the ghee used to make the laddus was adulterated and contained fish oil, beef tallow, and pig lard among other things. Devout Hindus across the world have been outraged even as politics continues over the issue. Â Read Full Story What are the two seemingly paradoxical sentiments? First, a huge majority of Indians believe that this adulteration is a deliberate act to hurt the Hindu faith. A shade more than six out of every ten respondents share this sentiment. Yet, when asked if the government should not run temples and hand them over to the devotees as is the practice in other religions in India, the proportion of INDIA bloc supporters who favour the move is just marginally higher than those who disapprove. text: There was an uncanny similarity in Uttar Pradesh's political optics between the inauguration of the 341-km Purvanchal Expressway and 302-km Agra-Lucknow Expressway. Back then, the Indian Air Force's aircraft had made a touch-and-go and put up a fighter jet show. On Tuesday, Prime Minister Narendra Modi landed on the airstrip on the Purvanchal Expressway. Both events were held in the second half of November, barely three months ahead of the Uttar Pradesh Assembly polls. Back then, Chief Minister Akhilesh Yadav was in attendance and his father and Samajwadi Party's topmost leader Mulayam Singh Yadav was the big political pull. On Tuesday, when incumbent Yogi Adityanath eyes another term in power in Uttar Pradesh, PM Narendra Modi was the bigger political pull for the ruling Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP). title: Babul Supriyo in TMC: Politics of opportunism. Ideology be damned
text: People may recall the movie The Ten Commandments in which Moses says to the pharaoh, “We are to be governed by God’s law, not by you.” In effect, he is saying we will not be governed by a person unless that person embodies the law. In India, the main political ideologies as around the world remain a tussle between the Left and the Right wing of the spectrum. Any political science student would have read the ideology of communism, fascism, liberalism, nationalism, feminism, capitalism, power politics and identity politics. Read Full Story A political ideology holds one in good stead as it provides the moral compass of what is right and what rots the society and how one can be that lone harbinger of change or crusader of truth. It’s a truth universally known that little gets done unless until one engages in ‘power politics’, the idea of sitting in the opposition is not exactly an appetising idea for our recent home spun ‘turncoats’. The people who carry the ideology of ‘no space’ are actually representatives of the ideology of the ‘ruling space’, as they continue to join hands with the ruling formation in power ditching old camps. Informed party sources say his thinking on Rajasthan imbroglio has been crisp, away from the usual Ashok Gehlot versus Sachin Pilot narrative. Read Full Story In Rahul Gandhi’s scheme of things, the Congress's ability to win back Rajasthan in November-December 2023 is paramount. By a similar measure, he is keen that the party manages to win a majority of Lok Sabha seats in 2024 Lok Sabha election. In 2019, almost six months after Gehlot was sworn in as Rajasthan chief minister, the grand-old-party drew a blank in 25 parliamentary seats. The Congress insiders say Rahul Gandhi is not most impressed when incumbent chief ministers keep winning by-polls but fail or falter during the assembly and parliamentary elections. title: Is the Sitalkuchi firing leading to further polarisation in West Bengal?
text: West Bengal is in the middle of perhaps the most polarised election it has ever seen. And the Sitalkuchi incident may have only added fuel to the fire. On April 10, a clash broke out between locals and CISF personnel at Sitalkuchi in Cooch Behar district. The central forces opened fire, resulting in the death of four civilians. The CISF alleged that the mob tried to snatch their weapons. Incidentally, all the four victims belonged to the Muslim community. Read Full Story The incident has led to a war of words between the Trinamool Congress and BJP. While Mamata Banerjee termed it “genocide”, leaders of the saffron camp too made explosive statements on the issue. Bengal BJP president Dilip Ghosh warned of more Sitalkuchis if people try to take law in their own hands. BJP leader Rahul Sinha said not four, but eight people should have been killed by CISF. No doubt, the Sitalkuchi incident will influence the remaining four phases of elections in Bengal. Denied entry in the area, Mamata spoke to the victims’ families over phone. The Congress had a government in Puducherry before the assembly election but lost it due to defections. Now, it has lost power in the state. title: Stalin rises in Tamil Nadu amidst the AIADMK cloud cover
text: The 'sun' rose in Tamil Nadu after a decade but not with the intensity it was expected to have. Yes, MK Stalin, for long called the Prince-in-waiting, will finally be chief minister. But he is too canny a politician to realise that Tamil Nadu has not been liberal with its votes for the DMK, the party of the rising sun. Frankly, the DMK's victory was never in question. The only point of discussion was how much would be the victory margin. In his assessment after voting day on 6 April, poll strategist for the DMK, Prashant Kishor had given the DMK-led alliance 205 seats. The AIADMK strategists, on the other hand, believed that the final numbers would be a reversal of the 2016 verdict when the AIADMK won 136 and the DMK alliance 98. Most exit polls had given 175 seats as the lower end of the band for the DMK. Which is why the DMK's final tally of 157 is underwhelming. title: Mamata vs BJP: Who had the edge in Bengal's close-contest seats?
text: While analysing their defeat in West Bengal, BJP leaders have repeatedly said that they lost several close-contest seats that sealed their fate in the elections. However, data suggests this is not true. In Bihar last year, 52 of the 243 assembly seats (21 per cent) witnessed close contests, with the margin of victory below 5,000 votes. In Bengal, the number stood at 36 out of 292 seats (12 per cent). Read Full Story Bengal witnessed polling in 292 of the 294 seats in eight phases, the results of which were declared on May 2. The incumbent Trinamool Congress won 213 seats with a vote share of 48 per cent, while the BJP managed to bag 77 seats with a vote share of 38 per cent. text: As the Haryana Assembly elections draw closer, the battle for the Dalit vote is heating up with the Bahujan Samaj Party and the Azad Samaj Party entering the poll fray. While Abhay Chautala’s Indian National Lok Dal has allied with Mayawati, Dushyant Chautala’s Jannayak Janta Party is teaming up with Chandrasekhar Azad in a bid to woo the Scheduled Caste voters. Read Full Story The INLD will contest 53 seats, and the BSP 37, including all SC reserved seats. On the other hand, the JJP will contest 70 seats and the ASP 20. title: Rahul unwilling, Sonia disinclined, will Kamal Nath assume a national role in Congress?
text: That the Gandhi trio Sonia, Rahul and Priyanka Gandhi are not power wielders, but trustees of power is becoming increasingly clear. For the past 48 hours, the Gandhis have been holding open-ended discussions with various “stakeholders”, perhaps scouting for someone to run the party, manage crisis, elections, raise funds and interact with non-NDA parties in the run-up for 2024. There are no briefings or even inspired leaks forcing incessant but highly speculative media reportage. Read Full Story A few things are, however, becoming evident. Firstly, Rahul Gandhi may not return as president of the All India Congress Committee (AICC). More significantly, Congress president Sonia Gandhi seems disinclined to continue even for a day beyond August 10 when she will be completing two years as ‘interim party president’. As per the Congress party’s constitution, the interim president’s tenure is capped for a year. A TECHNICAL CLAUSE This is one area where constitutional provisions of the party come into the play. Article XVIII [h] of the Congress’s constitution stipulates election of a ‘regular president’ by the AICC if the party president resigns. text: Chinks have appeared within farmer unions primarily on whether or not the agitation should be apolitical and Delhi-centric. Almost all farmer unions favoured strengthening the agitation in Delhi, but vested interests caused a split in some states, including Uttar Pradesh and Haryana. While the Kisan Mahapanchayat organised at Muzaffarnagar is part of Rakesh Tikait's strategy to flex muscles in Uttar Pradesh, the Karnal stalemate speaks volumes about BKU (Haryana) chief Gurnam Singh Chaduni's political ambitions. text: In January 2017, actor Sarath Kumar had slammed Kamal Haasan ahead of the elections to the South Indian Film Artists Association or the Nadigar Sangam. His charge was that Kamal was creating a rift between actors and breaking the Nadigar Sangam. The angst was because the Universal Hero, as Kamal is popularly referred to, had supported the team opposing Sarath in the elections. Read Full Story Like in films, a happy ending is when all is forgotten. Four years later, Sarath is now in talks with Kamal to strike a political deal in the Tamil Nadu elections. With a 3.72 per cent vote share in the 2019 Lok Sabha elections - the debut outing of the Makkal Needhi Maiam (MNM) - Kamal has emerged as the pivot of a possible Third Front in the Tamil Nadu political theatre. title: Will BJP's 'Parivartan Yatra' change its electoral fortune in Bengal?
text: To consolidate its presence, activate its army of booth level workers, galvanise uncertain and nervous voters, the Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP) has pulled out its oldest political exercise - the 'Rath Yatra', that will set off in five different directions across West Bengal, starting February 6, flagged off by the party's president JP Nadda and Home Minister Amit Shah. Insisting the 'Rath Yatra', for which permission was sought, was a "clerical error," the BJP has corrected the mistake and is all set to launch the 'Parivartan Yatra'. In doing so, BJP has achieved exactly what it thinks is needed in poll-bound West Bengal; shift gears in a campaign that was stuck in repeating the same rhetoric that with which it had successfully repositioned itself as the principal challenger to the incumbent two-term Trinamool Congress (TMC) government. Once a thriving business hub, it has attracted a number of trading communities such as Gujaratis and Marwaris, and a strong labour force from Bihar and Uttar Pradesh. Over the years, these communities have made West Bengal their home. title: Mandal not kamandal driving 2022 Uttar Pradesh Assembly polls
text: By fielding Chief Minister Yogi Adityanath from his home turf Gorakhpur not Ayodhya, in its first list of candidates, the Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP) perhaps missed creating that one big splash moment of 2022 Uttar Pradesh Assembly polls. However, there exist some very compelling reasons why the saffron party that has thrived on Hindutva elements, episodes and movements didn’t go for an easy-to-deploy move from the kamandal (religious) playbook and instead, through its first list of candidates for Uttar Pradesh polls, chose to stick to the “Mandal” or caste playbook. Read Full Story ALSO READ | Akhilesh Yadav to contest UP Polls, his first state election: Sources In this UP election, there is no dearth of low-grade incendiary kamandal or Hindutva elements. Till 2017, the BJP had only Prime Minister Narendra Modi as the Hindutva face. Now, there is saffron-clad Yogi. The Ram Mandir is under construction in the Ayodhya backyard. PM Modi through some high-profile visits for events like Kashi Vishwanath Corridor inauguration has kept the “dharma bhi vikas bhi” (religion and development together) theme alive. While the Samajwadi Party-led INDIA bloc will aim to maintain its momentum from the Lok Sabha polls, the BJP-led NDA will look to recover lost ground. But the buzz that this was only a smokescreen to engage with the BJP leadership in the national capital has refused to die down. title: Opinion: After 0/40 score in Lok Sabha Exam, AIADMK skips Vikravandi bypoll Test
text: The AIADMK ego is bruised after failing to open its account in the Lok Sabha elections in Tamil Nadu. Worse, the NDA secured the 2nd position in 12 constituencies, pushing the AIADMK to 3rd spot. Even more humiliating was the verdict from Kanyakumari and Puducherry, where even Seeman's NTK overtook the AIADMK, pushing it to fourth place. The immediate fallout of the 0/40 score is that the AIADMK has decided not to contest the byelection to the Vikravandi assembly constituency on July 10. The seat had become vacant after DMK legislator N Pugazhendhi's demise due to illness in April. The official reason cited by AIADMK supremo Edappadi Palaniswami is that the election won't take place in a “free and fair manner”. He alleged that the ruling party would misuse state machinery and “commit atrocities and irregularities, using money and muscle power”. He said the party had based its decision on the experience in the Erode (East) assembly bypoll in 2023 where he claimed voters had been bribed. To make it seem that he was taking a leaf from his late leader J Jayalalithaa's book, EPS pointed out how she had boycotted bypolls in constituencies like Cumbam and Bargoor in 2009

title: Opinion: Battleground Bihar
text: The battle for Bihar has taken an interesting turn with Prime Minister Narendra Modi holding a roadshow in Patna with Chief Minister Nitish Kumar on his side, seeking votes for the Bharatiya Janata Party candidate Ravi Shankar Prasad. Nineteen seats in the state have voted in the first four phases, and 21 will be voting in the remaining three. The BJP-led National Democratic Alliance swept the state, bagging 39 of the 40 seats in 2019, and this is a “maintain” state for the party and key to government formation. The turnout here in all phases in Bihar has been lower than the national average. text: In the last week or so, I've been repeatedly asked a question that's pregnant with all kinds of possibilities. Is the 2024 general election turning away from the BJP? You see, the general impression a month ago was that the 2024 elections were a done deal, that nothing could stop a Modi-led BJP from a hattrick of wins. But now, low voter turnout in some parts of the country, ground-level anti-incumbency, the Prime Minister’s increasingly strident speeches, references to Adani, Ambani and black money, jittery markets have convinced more than a few people –mainly those who, dare I say, desperately want Prime Minister Modi to be defeated and see an end of 'Modi Raj' – that this election is turning out to be a bit like 2004. title: Rajya Sabha polls: Indirect process, direct implication and ‘last resort’ politics
text: Elections for 57 Rajya Sabha seats across 15 states are slated for June 10. Results will be declared the same day. These polls were scheduled because 57 Rajya Sabha members are retiring. But effectively, polls will be held only for 16 seats in four states Maharashtra, Rajasthan, Karnataka, and Haryana after 41 seats were declared elected unopposed on June 5 because nobody else was contesting there. Read Full Story When Lok Sabha elections happen, all adult Indian citizens are eligible to vote in choosing which party or coalition will rule the country. Though Rajya Sabha polls are indirect, in which Members of Legislative Assemblies (MLAs) vote, they have crucial political implications. THE RAJYA SABHA The Lok Sabha (lower house) and the Rajya Sabha (upper house) are the two houses of India’s Parliament. Currently, the Rajya Sabha has a sanctioned strength of 245 Members of Parliament (MPs). Of them, 233 are elected from states and Union Territories (UTs). India’s President nominates the rest from the fields of art, literature, science and social services. title: Who will be India’s next President?
text: India’s President does not exercise executive powers, but all executive decisions are carried out in her name. She is required by the Constitution to act on the advice of the council of ministers led by the Prime Minister. But the President can ask the government to reconsider actions and offer advice. In matters of legislation, for example. So, it would be wrong to say that the role is only ceremonial or that the President is a mere figurehead or rubber stamp. Read Full Story Presidents like Pranab Mukherjee have been quite assertive, especially while dealing with mercy petitions from death row convicts. One of the most crucial roles of the President is seen when no party is able to get a parliamentary majority in a national election. So, the presidential election is crucial, and you should care about it. The election is indirect, but the result does indicate how much popular support both camps, the government and the opposition, have in the country. But it has seemingly failed to rise to the occasion adequately. In 2002, the BJP-led ruling National Democratic Alliance (NDA) fielded India's 'Missile Man', APJ Abdul Kalam (a Muslim). During the last election in 2017, the coalition picked then-Bihar Governor Ram Nath Kovind (a Dalit). Given the NDA's penchant for political import and candidates that mean support even from some of the opposition constituents, a tribal face was a strong possibility this time. title: Rishi Sunak will make good PM, shows UK opinion poll of Tory voters
text: Almost half of the voters for the UK's governing Conservative Party believe Rishi Sunak will make a good Prime Minister, according to the results of a new opinion poll on Sunday. ‘The Sunday Telegraph’ reports that the JL Partners poll of more than 4,400 people found that 48 per cent of those who backed the Tories in the 2019 general election felt the British Indian former Chancellor would be a good Prime Minister. Read Full Story This is also the first poll that puts Foreign Secretary Liz Truss in second place, with 39 per cent backing her for Prime Minister and 33 per cent in favour of Trade Minister Penny Mordaunt. The survey comes as the race to elect Boris Johnson’s successor as the next Conservative Party leader is narrowing down to the three main contenders, with former equalities minister Kemi Badenoch and Parliament’s Foreign Affairs Committee Chair Tom Tugendhat in fourth and fifth place, respectively. title: In post-Azad Congress, Tharoor buzz for prez post opens up leadership race
text: Two recent developments- Ghulam Nabi Azad’s exit and speculation on Shashi Tharoor running for the Congress president post- have set the power corridors abuzz in the Grand Old Party. Ghulam Nabi Azad’s exit from the Congress has exposed the Gandhis to huge internal disquiet which is essentially not a rebellion but a scenario fraught with bargaining, pressure and decentralisation of power. Read Full Story Congress MP from Thiruvananthapuram Tharoor has created a buzz about declaring his candidacy as he called for a contest with “several candidates” for the coming party presidential polls. A section of the Congress feels that Tharoor’s article in Mathrubhumi assumes significance. ALSO READ | Not dua, only dawa can save Congress: Ghulam Nabi Azad Sonia, Rahul and Priyanka are abroad for the AICC internal chief’s medical check-up. In the absence of an effective manager or a cogent counter strategy, Congress house stands divided, bitter, witnessing a lot of drift and indecisiveness. There is no dearth of senior leaders or crisis managers such as Manmohan Singh, AK Antony, Kamal Nath, Ambika Soni, Digvijaya Singh, Bhupesh Baghel, Ashok Gehlot and others, but there is neither an institutional mechanism nor a directive to grapple with the state of affairs.

title: Modi vs who? Opposition dreams of unity but is it even possible?
text: “Biharis will say Nitish Kumar, Bengalis will say Mamata Banerjee, Delhiites will say Arvind Kejriwal and South Indians will say KCR they will fight among themselves and Narendra Modi will be reelected as prime minister in 2024." The above was said in a recent meme posted on Twitter by a pro-BJP person. Memes are, well, memes. They lack the political seriousness of, say, a nuanced analysis and are made to make fun of one group while pushing the agenda of the other. Read Full Story Such posts are circulating on social media after Bihar Chief Minister and JDU leader Nitish Kumar and his Telangana counterpart K Chandrashekar Rao or KCR of the TRS addressed the media in Patna. Videos from the press conference went viral as journalists pressed for their views on who could be PM Modi’s challenger in the national polls. While an awkward Nitish wanted to avoid the questions and leave with KCR, the latter kept making him sit, looking eager to answer but mostly beating about the bush. Unlike KCR, Nitish has not made his prime ministerial ambitions public. But both leaders have been individually meeting other opposition leaders to build unity against the BJP-led NDA government

title: Fissures in Opposition derailing Nitish Kumar’s Delhi dream
text: Like a marathon runner, Nitish Kumar has been phasing his run for Delhi. There has been no Usain Bolt-like dash to the finishing tape but a visible increase in “cadence”- the number of steps one takes in a minute of running. But there is also a huge degree of predictability in what Nitish is doing or how he is doing it. The NDA would have never chosen him as its PM candidate. For the Opposition, he would have remained a political untouchable as long as he stayed in power with the BJP’s support. Read Full Story By dumping and hurting the BJP in Bihar, he made himself “acceptable” to the spectrum of Opposition parties and thus “eligible” for a national role. To officially signal that he is “available” to be “chosen” by the Opposition, Nitish arrived in India’s political hotspot - Delhi - on Monday. In the last three days, his ideological flexibility was on display. With handshakes, hugs and nostalgic rewinds to his old associations with Opposition parties and leaders, he plugged himself back into the Opposition system and went for a reboot. It was easy as the fragmented opposition needs a positive unity narrative. He had high-profile meetings

title: What is a fair Congress president poll that some party leaders are demanding?
text: Sonia Gandhi and her son, Rahul Gandhi, have been running the Congress for 24 years. The poll to elect a new party president, with a five-year term, has been delayed for three years since Sonia became the interim chief in 2019. The last election, with more than one candidate, was in 2000 when Jitendra Prasada unsuccessfully fought against Sonia. The Congress’s explanation has been that the Gandhis remain exceptionally popular among its leaders and workers. The party's stand is that all Congress leaders have always been free to contest. Read Full Story On the other hand, Congress rivals say these elections are a farce. Be that as it may, the party is gearing up for the poll. The poll has been postponed for some time due to Rahul’s Bharat Jodo Yatra, in which top Congress leaders are walking with him from Kanyakumari to Kashmir, but it is likely to happen in the coming weeks. The intent to hold the poll to elect the new Congress president is good. However, something else is also happening, which is not common in Congress culture. title: What’s behind Mamata Banerjee’s change of heart for PM Modi
text: Multiple anti-BJP leaders have harboured dreams of becoming Prime Minister Narendra Modi’s challenger. But only two of them have so far had tales to tell, having done something electorally spectacular to be in the running. If AAP’s Arvind Kejriwal surpassed his own party’s expectations and retook Delhi in 2020 against the combined might of the BJP’s big two, Mamata Banerjee of the TMC did the same the next year in West Bengal, humbling many psephologists who had projected a lotus bloom powered by PM Modi and Union minister Amit Shah’s no-holds-barred election campaign. Read Full Story While the Delhi chief minister projected himself to be the only true alternative of the prime minister in 2024 when India votes, his West Bengal counterpart spearheaded efforts to build opposition unity, though her aspirations for the country’s top job were subtler. But both had been equally vocal in accusing the ruling BJP at the Centre of letting loose central investigative agencies on opposition leaders to settle political scores. title: Sitaram Kesri, the Congress president who had his dhoti pulled
text: The morning of March 14, 1998, was rather pleasant. Neither too hot nor too cold. But those present at the All India Congress Committee (AICC) headquarters at 24, Akbar Road in Delhi betrayed distinct coldness. When party president Sitaram Kesri entered the Congress Working Committee (CWC) meeting, he felt something was off. Hardly anyone stood up to greet him. Kesri’s face turned red-hot when Pranab Mukherjee thanked him for his services and moved a resolution, asking Sonia Gandhi to become the AICC president. Kesri got up and left for his office, rejecting all the persuasion. It was his emotional way of holding onto something that was soon to become Sonia’s. Some accounts say Kesri was locked inside a bathroom for hours until Sonia took over the party. Later in the day, when Kesri was leaving, some youth Congress workers heckled him and even tried to pull his dhoti. Read Full Story But why are we suddenly talking about Sitaram Kesri? title: Drama of the last Congress poll in 2000 when Sonia got 99% of votes
text: In 1998, Sonia Gandhi became the Congress president for the first time. It was a bloodless coup that saw Sitaram Kesri’s unceremonious removal from the post and his belongings being sent away from the party office in a jeep, with a message for him never to return. But just when many thought a Gandhi was well and truly back in charge of the party, trouble began. Read Full Story The next year, Sharad Pawar, PA Sangma and Tariq Anwar mounted an open rebellion. They felt the Italian-born widow of former prime minister Rajiv Gandhi was not fit enough to run the country due to her foreign origins and limited political experience. The trio was expelled from the party. Now, from the post-Indira Gandhi era, only two stalwarts could challenge her. One was former Rajesh Pilot, who had been a minister under prime ministers Rajiv Gandhi and PV Narsimha Rao. Despite losing the Congress presidential election to Kesri in 1996, Pilot remained a front-line Congress leader. The other challenger, though not as potent, was Jitendra Prasada. title: Modi vs who? Why effort to revive Janata politics is like flogging a dead horse
text: About 10 days ago, a water-proof tent was erected in Haryana’s Fatehabad where Bihar Chief Minister and JD(U) leader Nitish Kumar attended a rally organised by INLD leader Om Prakash Chautala to mark the 109th birth anniversary of his father and former deputy prime minister Chaudhary Devi Lal. Leaders such as NCP’s Sharad Pawar, CPI(M)’s Sitaram Yechury and Sukhbir Singh Badal of the Shiromani Akali Dal were also in attendance, calling for all opposition parties to come together against the ruling BJP in the general elections. Read Full Story In the background, interactions also took place on expediting efforts to unite leaders of parties which came out of the erstwhile Janata Dal, to unite and possibly merge them ahead of 2024. Haryana’s clarion call followed Nitish’s meetings with non-BJP leaders, from K Chandrashekar Rao of the TRS to AAP’s Arvind Kejriwal, to build unity and collectively take on Prime Minister Narendra Modi. title: How KCR is chasing his national dreams with Bharat Rashtra Samithi
text: Eyeing the 2024 parliamentary elections, Telangana chief minister and the founder of Telangana Rashtra Samithi (TRS), K Chandrashekar Rao, on Wednesday renamed his party Bharat Rashtra Samithi (BRS). KCR has been a staunch advocate of a non-Congress, non-BJP opposition in the centre. In a bid to secure this, a resolution to change the TRS to BRS was passed by the party’s general body meeting in Hyderabad today. TRS workers raised slogans like “Desh ka neta KCR,” “Dear India, he is coming,” and “KCR is on the way” around where the general body meeting took place. Read Full Story The two-decade-old TRS was founded in 2001 with one single agenda- carving out a separate Telangana from Andhra Pradesh. In 2014, he achieved this goal. By renaming his party and announcing his intention to go at it at the national level, KCR has started the clock in the race to the 2024 general elections. The next twelve months will be pivotal and the party's performance in the 2023 Telangana assembly polls could decide whether it will receive cooperation from regional parties in its quest to become a non-traditional opposition.
