title: Why Tharoor should win Congress presidential poll but Kharge will
text: The October 17 Congress presidential election is the party’s internal matter. So why are we talking about it? Well, it’s important that we do. Because the new president will have a role to play, from a limited but significant role to just some kind of a role, depending upon who among the two candidates wins. That role will be a factor in deciding whether India’s principal opposition party comes out of its existential crisis or slips deeper into it. Read Full Story Talking generally, and not necessarily with a particular election in mind, the role of a robust opposition is crucial as it decides how well any democracy functions. Principally, it determines whether bills are passed in Parliament in noisy and chaotic automation or whether Parliament passes those bills with adequate consultation. A robust opposition more effectively raises the voices of those who did not vote for the party or coalition that won, holds the government of the day to account and enforces course correction whenever necessary. The Congress should be commended for at least holding an election. It’s also a shrewd political move to blunt the BJP’s attack on the Congress over the dynasty issue. Be that as it

title: Why Gujarat and other coming state polls don’t really hold the key to 2024
text: On Friday, the Election Commission of India announced the Himachal Pradesh Assembly election dates. Gujarat will also go to the polls by the year-end. Then, in 2023, elections will be held in Tripura, Meghalaya, Nagaland, Karnataka, Chhattisgarh, Madhya Pradesh, Mizoram, Rajasthan, and Telangana. The poll cycle is such that these state elections happen before India votes to elect its new central government. This is the time of the year when we get to read and see news reports about how these state polls are semi-finals before the big battle and how they’re the road to Delhi Darbar. Experts tell us how these state elections set the mood/tone for the national polls. Read Full Story How much of that is true? Well, the mood/tone part has some truth to it. For example, if the ruling party at the Centre loses in most states, it does galvanise the opposition and lends some clarity as to which leaders have the more bargaining power to be prime ministerial candidates. title: Congress presidential election 2022: Does it really matter who will win?
text: Congress is all set to get its first non-Gandhi president in over 24 years. But does it really matter who will win - Shashi Tharoor or Mallikarjun Kharge? Over 9,500 Pradesh Congress Committee (PCC) delegates across India cast their vote on Monday to elect a new party president, choosing between Mallikarjun Kharge and Shashi Tharoor to replace Sonia Gandhi. Shashi Tharoor has pitched himself as a candidate of change and emphasised that there is a need for a change in how the party functions, while Mallikarjun Kharge is known as the 'unofficial official candidate' over his unwavering loyalty to the Gandhi family. text: Former US President Donald Trump has announced that he will seek the Republican presidential nomination in 2024, aspiring to become the first person to take the White House after being rejected by voters in decades. He has filed paperwork with the Federal Election Commission declaring his candidacy. So, is Trump a Republican candidate already? No. The paperwork only allows him to raise campaign money. text: Punarvasu, a banker who lives in Delhi’s Dwarka area, has been affected by issues of civic mismanagement for years. “Because of the illegal parking menace, the neighbourhood lanes are crammed. One large vehicle rolls in and there’s no space even for pedestrians to walk. There’s no fumigation to check mosquito breeding. The state of waste management, look at the drains and landfills, is known to all. I can go on and on,” he said. title: What made Delhi’s AAP go to fight BJP in Modi’s Gujarat
text: The 10-year-old Aam Aadmi Party (AAP), with its governments in Delhi and Punjab and two MLAs in Goa, needs only six per cent votes/two seats in Gujarat or Himachal Pradesh to become a national party. Currently, there are eight national parties: BJP, Congress, CPI, CPM, BSP, NCP, TMC and NPP. In case of a review of footprint, CPM and BSP are likely to lose their national party status. Read Full Story The results for Gujarat and Himachal Pradesh will be announced on December 8. While the exit polls are not favourable for the AAP in the hill state, the party is predicted to win 10-20 per cent votes/10-20 seats on PM Narendra Modi’s turf. A good show in Gujarat, despite a record projected win for the BJP, will cap the AAP’s performance in the Municipal Corporation of Delhi (MCD) election where the exit polls have predicted (the results will be out today) a roaring victory for the party over the BJP in power for 15 years. title: AAP sprints to national party status. What will change now?
text: When Arvind Kejriwal launched the Aam Aadmi Party (AAP) in 2012, many dismissed it as a fly-by-night venture. He had no political experience. He was a former tax official, an RTI activist, and the driver and beneficiary of Anna Hazare’s anti-corruption movement in 2011. During these 10 years, two things happened: BJP’s surge and Congress’s decline. And the link between the two has been AAP’s emergence as a national political player. Leaders such as Raghav Chadha and Sanjay, who are seen in Parliament, are Rajya Sabha members. The other criterion is that the aspirant must have state party recognition in four states. For state party recognition, a party needs six per cent votes/two seats in a state assembly election, or three seats if its vote share is less than six per cent. This is where AAP scores. title: Reimagining Rahul Gandhi: Can Congress RaGa resonate with India in 2024?
text: Writing about Rahul Gandhi has increasingly been a tricky task, so much has been said about the Congress leader, right from his electoral debut in 2004 to now when he’s creating a buzz through his Bharat Jodo Yatra. In between, his party and supporters have pushed numerous coming-of-age and Rahul 2.0 stories. In contrast, the BJP and its supporters have left no word unspoken in branding him the pappu of Indian politics—a childish, non-serious, frivolous and visionless entitled scion not fit to lead the country or offer any good to its people. Read Full Story The Bharat Jodo Yatra has only intensified both narratives, especially from Rahul’s camp that's portraying the image of a loving, compassionate man of big ideas who fears nobody, who hates nobody despite suffering massive personal losses. A man who can address religious and caste tensions and economic inequalities and take India to great heights of unity and prosperity. This image has been found authentic and lapped up by large sections of people, primarily those who want PM Modi to go. But who is the real Rahul Gandhi amid all of this? How did he come to attract such conflicting attributes? Let’s begin in medias res.

title: Nine crucial state elections in 2023. What’s at stake?
text: 2023 will be a year when nine states and possibly the Union Territory of J&K will witness Assembly elections. These polls assume more importance because Lok Sabha elections will be held in 2024. These state polls will set the tone and mood for the big battle.Â If the BJP loses in many states, for example, it will galvanise the opposition and lend some clarity as to which leaders have the most bargaining power to be prime ministerial candidates. title: Opinion: Lok Sabha 2024
text: As both the NDA and INDIA blocs start announcing candidates, all eyes are on Maharashtra, which will go to polls in five phases for its 48 seats. Maharashtra is among the top five states that send the highest number of MPs to the Lok Sabha. In 2014 and 2019, the Bharatiya Janata Party-Shiv Sena combine won 41 out of the state’s 48 seats. But the political landscape of Maharastra has changed now. Read Full Story The Shiv Sena joined bete noire Indian National Congress and the Nationalist Congress Party to form the Maha Vikas Aghadi government in Maharashtra after talks of rotational chief ministership with the BJP fell through in 2019. The Shiv Sena then witnessed a split, with a chunk led by Eknath Shinde joining hands with the BJP in 2022 and forming a new government. A year later, a faction led by Ajit Pawar of the NCP joined this government, forming a Mahayuti. Both factions have got the official symbol from the Election Commission of India.Â Maharashtra Battle 2024 NDA: BJP + Shiv Sena (Shinde faction) + NCP (Ajit Pawar faction) versus INDIA: INC + Shiv Sena (Uddhav faction) + NCP (Sharad Pawar faction) The new political realignments

title: Opinion: Lessons for India’s Opposition from Erdogan’s big defeat in local Turkey polls
text: The recent local elections in Turkiye (earlier called Turkey) have caused a stir far beyond the country's borders, attracting the attention of analysts and political parties worldwide, including India. The success of the Opposition, Republican People’s Party (CHP), in these elections presents a compelling picture from which its Indian counterpart, especially the Indian National Congress (INC), can learn a lot. In both Turkiye and India, countries with very different political landscapes, strongman leaders have emerged to shape the political discourse significantly. Turkish President Recep Tayyip Erdogan and Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi share similarities in their leadership style and ideological orientation, prompting many Indian writers like Shashi Tharoor to make frequent comparisons. Read Full Story However, it is the striking parallels between the Turkish CHP and the Indian Congress that offer fascinating insights, especially in terms of their historical legacy and their ongoing struggles to reclaim political power. Much like the CHP, Congress can attempt to revive itself in states, regroup, and reboot itself in strategic alliances in the ensuing Haryana and Maharashtra Assembly polls. If a trend to beat the BJP in states such as Delhi and Bihar (which go to polls after the 2024 Lok Sabha elections) emerges,

text: Politics appears to have gone off normal course in Odisha just before the 2024 General Elections. The dramatic twist in the tale is the possible alliance between the Biju Janata Dal (BJD) and Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP) even though seat-sharing talks between the two have not come to a successful conclusion yet. The alliance's expectations soared especially after senior leaders from both the BJD and BJP issued a slew of statements that have kept new political possibilities alive. BJP state president Manmohan Samal has undertaken four trips to Delhi in March alone. After Samal returned from his third trip on March 14, he tweeted that the BJP would fight the simultaneous Lok Sabha and Assembly polls alone without an alliance, claiming they would form the government in Odisha. Later, Samal deleted this tweet, clarifying to the media that the final decision on the alliance would be taken by the national party president and BJP Parliamentary Board. title: J&K polls: What election fever in winter chill means amid Bharat Jodo Yatra, Gupkar cracks and delimitation
text: The Bharat Jodo Yatra will set its foot on Jammu and Kashmir in the third week of January. Senior Congress leader Rahul Gandhi is going to spend eight days during the concluding phase of the march. The yatra reaches J&K at a very crucial time, with Assembly elections – if hints dropped by the BJP are anything to go by – to be held in the UT in May 2023 when the weather improves. Kashmiri parties have been demanding elections, saying the same have been delayed by the Centre for political reasons.Â Read Full Story Even as the Congress has exhorted "like-minded" groups and individuals to join the march, it could be a good platform for the grand old party to develop political proximity with parties that want to keep the BJP at bay—in the upcoming Assembly. The J&K Legislative Assembly was suspended after the BJP fell out with Mehbooba Mufti’s People’s Democratic Party (PDP) in June 2018. But between 2014 – when the BJP-PDP alliance was struck – and 2018 – when they split – much of political and demographic landscape in the Valley has shifted.Â Read |Â Is Rahul Gandhi's ‘evolution’ on the Bharat Jodo Yatra for real?

text: In August, Bihar Chief Minister Nitish Kumar dumped his partner, the BJP, and formed a new government with pre-poll rival RJD. His explanation was that in Bihar, the BJP was trying to do a Maharashtra where Shiv Sena leader Eknath Shinde had, two months ago, run away with most party MLAs to form a new government with the BJP, the pre-poll-ally-turned rival. RJD MLA and former Nitish minister Sudhakar Singh has called the CM a beggar, a night watchman and “likened” him to Mahabharata’s transgender character Shikhandi, saying the JDU leader’s heart lies with the BJP. title: 400 days for Lok Sabha polls: Why 2024 will be historic in many ways
text: Every time an election approaches, many of us choose words like historic to describe it. There’s not much wrong with such a description because elections, by their very nature, do alter history—even irrespective of the results. Because even when a party’s victory or loss in a national poll doesn’t fully represent the aspirations or anxieties of the whole country, they do mirror the rejection or endorsement of certain political philosophies. Read Full Story It’s through the election process that our beliefs either deepen or are called into question, shaping the present and future of a nation, and, of course, its people. ââ On Tuesday, Prime Minister Narendra Modi told his party, the BJP, that “We have 400 days before the 2024 Lok Sabha polls, and we have to do everything in people’s service. We have to create history.” So, what’s the history here that we’re talking about? Narendra Modi has won two national polls, in 2014 and 2019. Another win, in the May 2024 elections, will propel him into the big league as far as Indian prime ministers are concerned. title: Why a weak Opposition is not PM Modi’s biggest strength. It’s something else
text: Much is said about how a weak Opposition, fragmented by individual ambitions and personal acrimony, has been the fuel for the BJP’s unstoppable election machine. Some say a united Opposition can defeat the BJP because in the 2019 national election, about 60 per cent of India’s voting public did not back the party. It wasn’t much different in 2014. To be fair, all of them were not necessarily anti-BJP voters. Theoretically, some of them might have gone for the BJP if they didn’t have the choices that they had. They were certainly non-BJP voters, split among 40-odd parties. But some of this fragmentation is not going to change because of regional electoral complexities. title: Nitish versus Kushwaha: Why Janata politics never had a cure for its splitting headache
text: On Monday, senior Janata Dal (United) leader Upendra Kushwaha and his supporters quit the party and announced the formation of his own outfit: Rashtriya Lok Janata Dal. Kushwaha had been sulking after JD(U) leader and Bihar Chief Minister Nitish Kumar recently announced that his deputy, Tejashwi Yadav of the Rashtriya Janata Dal (RJD), would lead the ruling coalition in the 2025 legislative assembly election, seen to be the former’s last electoral hurrah. Read Full Story A prominent Other Backward Class (OBC) man, Kushwaha had seen himself as Nitish’s successor in Bihar’s politics. The development will benefit the BJP, a party Kushwaha has been close to and was a central minister between 2014 and 2018. The saffron party is still smarting from Nitish Kumar’s switch to what was Bihar’s Opposition bloc. Â But all this cannot be seen in isolation. In fact, it’s a story in a long series of splits that India’s Janata politics, bound initially by anti-Congressism and pro-backward class agendas, has suffered over the decades. To understand why many Janata splinters are today in bed with the Congress, we need to go back in time. Â ALSO READ |Â Unable to handle Bihar: BJP's swipe at Nitish Kumar

text: Recently, ‘Power Star’ Pawan Kalyan announced that his Jana Sena Party (JSP) would contest seven to 14 Assembly seats in the 2024 Telangana elections and reiterated that he had the support of the BJP. However, he also asserted that Jana Sena’s role in Telangana politics would be limited given that his party plays a much stronger role in Andhra Pradesh (AP). Right now, the JSP is in alliance with the BJP in AP and Telangana, but the turning point for JSP would be if Pawan Kalyan decides to form an alliance with the Telugu Desam Party (TDP), led by N Chandrababu Naidu, ahead of the polls. The BJP, however, is clear that they do not want to partner with the TDP, which leaves the JSP in a fix. title: Can BJP turn the tide in Southern Karnataka?
text: In the post-mandir era, the BJP has been a rising political force in India. Since then, the party’s support base has increased massively in central, north and western India. In the south, the BJP managed to grow significantly in Karnataka. This is the only state in southern India where the party formed the government, in 2008 and 2018. On both occasions, the BJP did not cross the majority mark in the Assembly but was the single-largest party in the House. Read Full Story The BJP’s rise in Karnataka began from coastal Karnataka and expanded to other regions. In the last few elections, the party has performed well across Karnataka except southern Karnataka (or Mysore region). Here, the BJP is weak and still behind the current Opposition: the Congress and the JD(S). However, the BJP did manage to improve its electoral support in the 2019 Lok Sabha election and in the 2023 assembly polls. The ruling party will have to repeat its performance in southern Karnataka to return to power in the state as this region holds 51 assembly seats. title: Why Rahul Gandhi's sentencing could also be good news for Congress
text: On Thursday, Rahul Gandhi was sentenced to two years in jail by a Surat court for referring to thieves as having the surname Modi. title: Amid Atiq’s killing row, why Bihar is pushing for Don Anand Mohan’s release
text: In 1994, gangster and Bihar People's Party (BPP) leader Chhotan Shukla was killed in a police encounter. Violence broke out during Chhotan’s funeral cortege and a blood-baying mob of thousands, led by (now-defunct) BPP founder and don Anand Mohan, dragged Dalit IAS officer and Gopalganj District Magistrate G Krishnaiah out of his car and lynched him. The 35-year-old innocent civil servant, born to a landless Dalit family in Andhra Pradesh, was thrashed, stoned and shot when RJD leader Lalu Yadav was Bihar’s Chief Minister. The country was shocked. Read Full Story Facing many other cases of serious crime over the years, Anand Mohan, a Tomar Rajput, was elected MP from the Seohar Lok Sabha seat in Bihar in 1996 while still in jail. In 2007, the Patna High Court sentenced Anand Mohan, son of a freedom fighter, to death for abetting the crime, making him the first Indian politician since Independence to get the death penalty and ending his political career as far as fighting polls went. The sentence was reduced to rigorous life imprisonment in 2008. title: What’s behind Oppn’s caste census chorus? Can it challenge PM Modi in 2024?
text: Congress leader Rahul Gandhi has asked the Modi government to conduct a caste census. Naturally, Congress president Mallikarjun Kharge has written to PM Modi, demanding that caste census be conducted immediately for meaningful social justice. Tamil Nadu's MK Stalin government and NCP leader Sharad Pawar, both Congress allies, have also urged the Centre to conduct caste census along with India's decennial census. Anti-BJP parties in states such as Bihar and Uttar Pradesh have made the same pitch. The list is getting longer by the day. Read Full Story First, what’s caste census? Caste census means the inclusion of caste-wise tabulation of India's population in the census exercise. India started counting its people in 1872. From 1952, the country also counted and published separate data on Scheduled Castes (SCs) and Scheduled Tribes (STs). India also publishes data related to the religions, languages and socio-economic status of its people. text: It is being argued for quite some time now, that parliamentary-style elections are increasingly becoming the choice of a leader. In the Lokniti-CSDS Post Poll Study in 2014, we found that one-fourth of those who voted for the BJP, did so because Narendra Modi was the Prime Minsterial candidate of the party. In 2019, this proportion jumped to one-third. This is clearly indicative of how Lok Sabha elections are more and more becoming the choice of the preferred Prime Minister. text: Quiz: He is seeking his third term. His regime is known to suppress dissent, stifle public discourse, display scant regard for political rights and civil liberties, pursue political opponents via government agencies and it goes after journalists, students, activists, human rights advocates arresting them by the dozen. His government has a poor economic record and most of his voters vote for him for narrow religious and hyper-nationalistic reasons. Known to be a good orator, he would like the country, slipping on various parameters of democracy, to transform into a monolithic political entity, squelching all Opposition spaces. Intolerant of criticism, his regime is heavily into micromanaging and throttling what is left of free speech online while the mainstream media, some of it controlled by his allies, lies supine and spread-eagled, broadcasting only the thunderous silence of surrender, and his heralders incessantly sing noisy hosannas to the One And Only. title: CM Bommai concedes defeat, says will re-organise Karnataka BJP
text: With the Election Commission's trends showing Congress leading in more than half the number of Karnataka Assembly seats, Chief Minister Basavaraj Bommai acceded to the BJP's defeat and said the party would carry a "detailed analysis". Congress, shortly after the counting of votes began on Saturday, showed trends breaching the BJP's lone southern citadel in Karnataka wherein more than half of the 224-member Assembly was in the grand old party's favour.Â A party or a coalition requires the magic number of 113 seats in the Karnataka Assembly to form the government in the state. Read Full Story "We (BJP) are not able to make up to the mark.Â Will do a detailed analysis, and we will see what the gaps and deficiencies are at different levels. We will re-organise the party and do well in the Lok Sabha polls," he said.Â Karnataka Election Results 2023 Live The trends showed the Congress leading against the BJP in what was seen to be a clear-cut contest. The state went to the polls in a single phase on May 10.Â In Bommai's constituency, Shiggaon,Â Congress has surged ahead in 117 seats while BJP leads in 75 seats as per the latest ECI trends.

title: Mandal 2.0 vs Kamandal: Has Congress got its political antidote for BJP’s Hindutva?
text: The astonishing victory in the Karnataka Assembly Election has opened the debate whether this result has provided a mantra to the Congress party for defeating the BJP and its Hindutva project in the upcoming assembly elections and the 2024 Lok Sabha elections. And that mantra is the revival of caste politics. This is because Rahul Gandhi invocated the slogan- ‘Jitni Abaadi, Utna Haq’ (Proportionate Reservation/Right) and demanded releasing the Socio-Economic and Caste Census (2011-12) during the election campaign on 16 April in Kolar, Karnataka. Congress President Mallikarjun Kharge also wrote a letter to the government to release the Socio-Economic and Caste Census conducted during the UPA -II regime. The opposition leaders, from Nitish Kumar, M. K. Stalin, Akhilesh Yadav to Tejashwi Yadav, have already been demanding the caste census. Read Full Story Rahul Gandhi’s slogan and demand are rooted in India's caste politics (Mandal politics), often seen as an antidote to the Hindutva politics of the BJP. We trace its genealogy and examine its implications on the outcome of the Karnataka election while critically investigating the possibilities and impossibilities of using this mantra in upcoming assembly elections and the 2024 Lok Sabha elections. title: Downfall of a behemoth: As 2024 approaches, Mayawati risks fading into political oblivion
text: The coming Lok Sabha polls next year may finally seal the fate of a national leader who just one and a half decades ago seemed poised to radically alter India’s political scenario. Mayawati, popularly known as Behenji, who has been steadily sliding backwards in recent years, appears to be heading towards a precipice that may well see her plunge into political oblivion in 2024. It is a telling example of the twists and turns of Indian politics that has led to the downfall of a behemoth aptly symbolised by the rapidly fading elephant symbol of her Bahujan Samaj Party (BSP). This had not only angered her and her supporters but also provoked rebuke from many political pundits who felt I was writing her political obituary too soon. text: The Chhattisgarh Assembly elections are nearing. And Congress seems to be ahead of other parties as it has officially begun holding divisional meetings across the state. text: Traditionally in India, most alliances and parties first get a name. Next, the acronym becomes a natural derivative. For example, the Congress-led alliance in 2004 was named United Progressive Alliance. And thus, the acronym UPA. The Bharatiya Jana Sangh made way for Bharatiya Janata Party and thus the BJP. In October 1997, a few Congressmen, unhappy with the mothership, formed the Akhil Bharatiya Loktantrik Congress or ABLC in Lucknow and joined hands with the Kalyan Singh government. They retained Congress in the party’s name and to improve their sales pitch, added Loktantrik (democratic). title: Opinion: Solution to the politics of hatred and the folly of last century
text: Insurgency has gripped the North East state of Manipur since the first week of May. It appears that the two ethnic groups are unwilling to co-exist. The Kukis, who make up 10 per cent of the population, occupy 50 per cent of the land and have a significant presence in the bureaucracy. On the other hand, the Meiteis, who constitute 56 per cent of the population, occupy only 10 per cent of the land but hold political power. Today, these indigenous groups seem intent on eliminating each other from Manipur, seemingly forgetting their shared homeland. Read Full Story In response, the apex court has appointed a three-member judicial committee headed by Justice Gita Mittal, with Justice Asha Menon and Shalini Joshi as the other members. The root of the current conflict lies in the verdict of the Manipur High Court on April 20. Justice MV Muralidharan directed the government to consider including the Meitei community in the Scheduled Tribe (ST) list. This decision sparked the insurgency within two weeks. This region has a history of violence dating back to the nineties, coinciding with the onset of globalisation. In fact, 400 people were killed in the Kuki-Naga clashes of 1993. Long

title: Opinion: 'One Nation, One Election' bill in Parliament? Here's what it means
text: A 'One Nation, One Election' bill, Uniform Civil Code, a women’s reservation bill, population control legislation, or a universal income scheme — what will feature in the special Parliament session convening between September 18 and 22? The suspense will likely end only when Speaker Om Birla convenes a meeting of the Business Advisory Panel, a 15-member group that includes members of the Opposition, where agenda papers are required to be circulated before the proposed session. Read Full Story A full-majority government is within its rights to advance Lok Sabha polls. It does not require any parliamentary sanction to dissolve the 17th Lok Sabha prematurely. The Special Parliamentary session, therefore, has to have some decorative or substantive legislative business, which needs about the next six months to fructify or bring electoral dividends. In such a scenario, the Uniform Civil Code or a universal income scheme would be high on optics. Meanwhile, the Women's Reservation Bill, population control legislation, and “One Nation, One Election” would come with the flip side and legal challenges. However, going by the media tizzy and sources-based speculation, simultaneous Assembly polls in as many as 12 states alongside the general elections seems high on the probability list. The

text: The last time Uma Bharti won an election in Madhya Pradesh was in 2003 when the Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP) trounced the Congress to end the 10-year rule of Digvijaya Singh. It was a big moment in the life of the mercurial politician, who was at the time considered a leading light of the Hindutva movement. A decade ago, the former chief minister was banished from her home state and made to contest an Assembly election in the Bundelkhand region of Uttar Pradesh in 2012 because leaders in Madhya Pradesh argued that her presence would destabilise the Shivraj Singh Chouhan government -- and the party. text: Telangana votes on November 30 in the final leg of semi-finals 2023. The southern state is witnessing a tough contest between the ruling Bharat Rashtra Samithi and Congress, with the grand old party threatening to dislodge the decade-long K Chandrashekar Rao government, riding on its poll guarantees and victory in neighbouring Karnataka. End of BRS rule? The BRS has long benefitted from being credited for the creation of Telangana. But is the honeymoon period over now? In 2014, the BRS (then, the Telangana Rashtra Samithi) won 63 seats in a house of 119, surpassing the simple majority by three seats. The Congress won 21. 12 MLAs of TDP merged with TRS in March 2016 providing a fillip to KCR. title: Opinion: Tale of takht and takhta
text: Dhaka had fallen. The rump Pakistan was in shock. But despite the 1971 war debacle, the military junta continued to believe that it could keep ruling the country. Generals were so detached from reality, so blind to the seething sentiment on the street — and within the rank and file of the Pakistan Army — that they couldn’t read the writing on the wall. Finally, the Yahya Khan-led military junta was forced out of office by the Pakistan Army, and replaced by a civilian martial law administrator, Zulfikar Ali Bhutto. A little over half a century later, Pakistan faces something similar once again. An unexpected election result has completely upended the carefully laid out plans of the General Headquarters of the Pakistan Army. The Bharatiya Janata Party, the captain of the NDA, enters the fray in good form having defeated Congress 3-0 in the Hindi heartland states that went to polls recently. The form of Congress, the uncrowned captain of the INDIA bloc, is of concern to the alliance partners, as it is going through a rough patch. Though, good news is that Congress is showing signs of revival in the crucial Southern India pitch which sends a quarter of MPs to Lok Sabha. Read Full Story Changes in circumstances, apart from the form of the two main players, has resulted in quite contrasting strategies for the BJP and Congress going into the 2024 polls. title: Opinion: Will Kharge be the man against Modi in 2024? A SWOT analysis
text: One of the most surprising aspects of the INDIA bloc meeting on Tuesday was the proposed choice of Mallikarjun Kharge as the coalition's prime ministerial candidate. While a broader consensus eludes Kharge within the INDIA alliance, his caste, experience and proximity to the Gandhis provide a ring of credibility around his name. At the ‘INDIA’ meeting, West Bengal Chief Minister Mamata Banerjee and Delhi Chief Minister Arvind Kejriwal proposed Kharge as the alliance’s prime ministerial candidate, according to MDMK chief Vaiko. Read Full Story Kharge managed to sound gung-ho even as he ducked. “Let’s win first, the PM face will be discussed later,” he told a press conference after the meeting. All eyes are now on Bahujan Samaj Party (BSP) supremo Mayawati. If Mayawati backs the idea of having the country’s first Dalit prime minister, the INDIA alliance story for 2024 would gain momentum. Uttar Pradesh is one state where the BJP won the bulk of seats in 2014 and 2019. title: Opinion: Forget 2023, even Lok Sabha 2024 outcome seems to be a foregone conclusion
text: Pundits and people who are of a certain ideological bent of mind are a die-hard lot; or you could say they are a never-say-die lot. Ordinary mortals who tremble with anger and disgust at the very mention of the name Narendra Modi would be in mourning today. The more emotional amongst them would be sharing WhatsApp texts anticipating the end of civilisation, and of the idea of India. Read Full Story The more level-headed amongst them would be looking closely at the numbers, wondering what electoral strategies the Congress and its INDIA partners need to adopt and execute to stop the Modi juggernaut in 2024. They are acutely aware of the fact that Jawaharlal Nehru remains the only prime minister to win three consecutive Lok Sabha mandates. They will do anything to prevent Modi (of all people) to equal that incredible record.Â But the die-hard lot is a different species. Even as the authors write this and even before the Election Commission has handed over certificates to victorious candidates, this lot is talking excitedly about an imminent repeat of 2003 and 2004 in 2023 and 2024.
